extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors (DS18S20, ±0.5°C accuracy), and one external temperature sensor. The system regulates temperature within the box regardless of content, with a heatbed capable of delivering 100W power (200J in 2s) to raise air temperature from 293K (20°C) to 300K (26.85°C). The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B serves as the controller, managing actuation of the heatbed, fan, and sensor data acquisition.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) provides monitoring (real-time temperature tracking via RabbitMQ), prediction (thermal inertia simulation with 4-parameter model), optimization (control logic for temperature regulation), and visualization (data sharing between controller and simulation models). It enables fault detection, state estimation, and supervisory control through calibrated models, with the ability to simulate and predict PT behavior under operational conditions. The DT integrates with the PT via 1-Wire bus and RabbitMQ for bidirectional data flow.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tools include Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (GPIO for sensor/fan control), RabbitMQ (open-source message broker for communication server), DS18S20 temperature sensors (1-Wire protocol), MOSFETs/optocouplers (for electrical isolation), PCB design (decoupling control signals), and open-source hardware/software repository (GitHub). Simulation engines use 2- and 4-parameter thermal models, with calibration algorithms updating models using sensor data. The 1-Wire bus enables multi-sensor connectivity via unique 64-bit serial codes.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message passing (100Hz data update frequency). Kalman filter state estimation with 2-second air circulation cycle. Calibration experiments use 2-second energy calculation intervals. Temporal granularity for temperature monitoring at 0.5°C accuracy with 1-Wire bus sampling.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Dual model instances (Model A: 2 parameters, Model B: 4 parameters) with hierarchical calibration. Centralized control architecture via Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. Decentralized sensor nodes (3 DS18S20 temperature sensors). Coordination mechanisms include model parameter synchronization and Kalman filter state correlation between physical and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture with RabbitMQ as message broker, Raspberry Pi controller, and PCB-based actuation. Resource management includes 4A MOSFET current handling and optocoupler isolation. System coordination uses self-adaptation loop with 8-step calibration workflow, including Kalman filter reconfiguration and what-if simulations for controller optimization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability via RabbitMQ AMQP protocol with open-source message brokering. Integration patterns include IoT device communication (1-Wire bus, GPIO interfaces) and CPS simulation co-simulation. Dependencies on external systems include Raspberry Pi hardware, DS18S20 sensors, and open-source PCB design tools. Data exchange uses MQTT/AMQP protocols for real-time monitoring and simulation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin computational models include behavioral (e.g., Model A/B for thermal inertia prediction) and analytical models (e.g., heat equations Q=cmΔT). Data sources: sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors), physical twin (PT) via RabbitMQ. Data management: calibration algorithms update models with parameters (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A). Models B includes additional parameters (C_heater, G_heater) for enhanced accuracy. Algorithms: Kalman filter for state estimation, co-simulation for what-if scenarios, and real-time data streaming to dashboards.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators: MOSFETs for heatbed control, optocouplers for signal isolation. Control mechanisms: Bang-bang controller with parameters (LL: lower temp limit, UL: upper temp limit, H: heating duration, C: waiting duration). Remote interfaces: Raspberry Pi via RabbitMQ. Safety constraints: Safe protocol in low-level driver to prevent overheating. Actuation ranges: 4A current handling via MOSFETs, temperature limits enforced by controller.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (accuracy: ±0.5°C, range: -55°C to 125°C). Spatial distribution: Three sensors in incubator. Sampling frequency: Not specified, but data transmitted via RabbitMQ. Data transmission: 1-Wire bus with unique 64-bit serial codes per sensor. Precision: 0.5°C accuracy in operational range (-10°C to 85°C). Data collection: Continuous temperature monitoring via low-level driver.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity: Model B (4 parameters) outperforms Model A (2 parameters) in thermal inertia prediction. Validation: Calibration experiments (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K for Model B) and co-simulation with PT data. Uncertainty sources: Sensor accuracy (±0.5°C), model assumptions (e.g., uniform temperature distribution). Error detection: Kalman filter for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance: Regular calibration, model verification against experimental data, and safety protocol enforcement.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors, fan airflow measurements) to digital twin via RabbitMQ message broker. Data types include temperature readings (°C, 0.5°C accuracy), airflow rates (0.02 m³/s), and power metrics (100W). Communication protocol: AMQP (RabbitMQ). Transmission frequency: Continuous data sampling with event triggers for temperature anomalies. Data preprocessing includes calibration (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K). Data integration mechanisms involve time series databases for dashboard visualization. Data quality assurance through Kalman filter state estimation (Model B) and model calibration experiments.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (temperature setpoints) sent via RabbitMQ to Raspberry Pi controller. Command formats: JSON-based messages with target temperatures. Validation mechanisms include safety range checks for heater power (100W max). Feedback systems use temperature sensors (3 DS18S20) and fan status. Conflict resolution not explicitly detailed but implied through Kalman filter anomaly detection. Command execution confirmation via RabbitMQ message acknowledgments and controller state updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Network infrastructure uses RabbitMQ (AMQP) for bidirectional communication between Raspberry Pi (edge node) and digital twin. Network topology: Client-server model with Raspberry Pi as client. Bandwidth requirements: Low-latency (<2s for air circulation) for real-time control. Reliability mechanisms include message persistence in RabbitMQ and redundant sensors. Security protocols: TLS encryption for RabbitMQ (implied by standard security practices). Redundancy: Multiple DS18S20 sensors for temperature monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment on Raspberry Pi 4 (edge computing) with Raspberry Pi as controller host. Computational requirements: ARM Cortex-A53 processor for control logic. Storage: Time series database for logged sensor data. Scalability: Modular design allows adding sensors or actuators. Deployment models: Edge-first with optional cloud integration for analytics. Availability assurance through redundant sensors and Raspberry Pi's reliable hardware.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages such as model creation (e.g., two simulation models for temperature prediction in the incubator case study), calibration (e.g., Model B with four parameters for thermal inertia prediction), and integration with Physical Twin (PT) data. Phases involve data collection from PT sensors, model updating via calibration algorithms, and monitoring/optimization. Transition management includes deploying models into DT frameworks and iterative refinement through self-adaptation loops (e.g., re-calibrating Model B when new objects are introduced). Governance involves open-source tooling (e.g., Raspberry Pi, RabbitMQ) and version control for software components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT development process uses methodologies like model-based simulation (e.g., differential equations for car dynamics), open-source hardware/software (e.g., Raspberry Pi, RabbitMQ server), and calibration experiments (e.g., optimizing sensor parameters). Quality assurance includes validation through co-simulations and anomaly detection via Kalman filters. Version control is achieved through open-source repositories. Evolution strategies involve iterative calibration (e.g., Model B reconfiguration), self-adaptation loops, and on-demand recalibration workflows.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters for heatbed state estimation, and what-if simulations via co-simulations (e.g., optimizing controller parameters). Analytics capabilities involve correlating measured data with non-measured heatbed temperatures. User interaction includes dashboard visualization and automated controller reconfiguration. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter accuracy and calibration validation (e.g., Model B's superior thermal inertia prediction).

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
